cards.text: Chapter 5 summary: Use {{c1::constexpr}} for compile-time constants and {{c2::const}} for runtime constants.
For read-only string function parameters, prefer {{c1::std::string_view}} over {{c2::const std::string&}}.

cards.front: What are the three types of constants covered in Chapter 5?
cards.back:
• Literals (fixed values in code)
  • const variables (runtime or compile-time)
  • constexpr variables (guaranteed compile-time)

cards.front: What are the key differences between std::string and std::string_view?
cards.back:
• std::string: owns the string data, can modify
  • std::string_view: read-only view, doesn't own data, lightweight
Use string for storage, string_view for parameters.

cards.question: Choose the best type for each variable:
cards.answer:
1. constexpr int maxStudents{ 30 };
2. const int age{ getAge() };
3. constexpr double pi{ 3.14159 };
4. std::string date{ getCurrentDate() };
cards.explanation: Use constexpr for compile-time constants, const for runtime constants, and regular variables for values that change.
